## Lab brief

Build and evaluate a character-level bigram language model for next-token
prediction. Start with a transparent count-based model, then extend it to a neural
version if time permits.

The original practical uses a plain-text names file named `data/names.txt` with one
name per line. If that file is distributed in class, use it. Otherwise use any
comparable list of names or short strings where character transitions are easy to
inspect.

## Tasks

1. Load and inspect the names corpus.
   - Report the number of names,
   - the shortest and longest length,
   - and the set of unique characters.
2. Build a count-based bigram model.
   - Add start and end tokens,
   - count every adjacent character pair,
   - and store the counts in a table or matrix.
3. Convert counts into probabilities.
   - Apply additive smoothing so unseen transitions do not get probability zero.
   - Inspect one row of the resulting probability matrix.
4. Generate synthetic names by sampling from the model.
   - Produce at least 10 names.
   - Comment on whether the generated names look plausible or only locally
     consistent.
5. Evaluate the model with average negative log likelihood.
   - Compute the loss on the observed corpus.
   - Explain what a lower loss means in this setting.
6. Compare against a naive baseline.
   - Use uniform random character sampling or another clearly weaker baseline.
   - Explain why the bigram model performs better.
7. If time permits, estimate the same next-character task with a simple neural
   network.
   - one-hot encode the input character,
   - learn a weight matrix with gradient descent,
   - compare the neural model loss with the count-based model.
8. Write a short reflection on what the model still cannot do.
   - Focus on short memory, data sparsity, or lack of semantic understanding.

## Starter outline

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

names_path = "names.txt"  # or the path provided by your instructor
words = open(names_path, encoding="utf-8").read().splitlines()
```

Do not hide the logic inside a library wrapper. The point of the lab is to make
next-token prediction visible.
